text.title: Ollama Tool Calling and Structured Outputs — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the ollama-tool-calling methodology. Sources: Ollama documentation — tools (function calling); Llama 3.1 tool-use cookbook; Pydantic JSON Schema docs.

rule.statement: Declare tool schemas with `additionalProperties: false`, explicit `type` for every field, and `required` lists; never rely on `type: any`.
rule.rationale: Quantised models will fill missing schema constraints with hallucinated keys, breaking downstream consumers.

rule.statement: Run a one-shot JSON repair (`json-repair`) on tool-call arguments before validating; only fail the call if repair still produces invalid JSON.
rule.rationale: Local models emit malformed JSON 5–15% of the time; one repair pass turns a hard failure into a soft self-correction.

rule.statement: Validate parsed tool arguments against the schema with `jsonschema.validate` before invoking the tool; surface validation errors back to the model with the rule that violated.
rule.rationale: Schema validation is the only mechanical defence against silent argument drift between model and tool.

rule.statement: Keep the active tool count ≤ 8 per turn; bundle related tools behind a router-tool when more are needed.
rule.rationale: Tool-selection accuracy degrades sharply past 8 tools on 7B–13B local models; a router-tool is a documented mitigation.

rule.statement: Attach a trace id to every tool call and log: tool name, arguments, result, latency, validation outcome.
rule.rationale: Without traces, tool-call regressions on local models are unreproducible — quantisation makes runs non-deterministic.
